_disclaimer: ALL ITEMS IN THIS FILE ARE SYNTHETIC PLACEHOLDERS, hand-authored to exercise the LSAT Item contract. They are NOT real LSAT items and must NOT be used as study material. Real items must be sourced from official LSAC PrepTests with redistribution licensing (D-SR11). Every item carries SyntheticFlag=SYNTHETIC.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Columnist: My opponent argues that lowering the speed limit will save lives. But when the neighboring state lowered its speed limit last year, its traffic fatalities did not decline at all. So the proposed reduction will not save lives here either.
items.ChoiceA: citing a comparable case in which the measure failed to produce the predicted effect.
items.ChoiceB: defining a key term more precisely than the opponent did.
items.ChoiceC: showing that the opponent's premises contradict each other.
items.ChoiceD: appealing to an authority on traffic safety.
items.ChoiceE: attacking the opponent's character rather than the proposal.
items.WhyWrongA: CORRECT. The columnist uses an analogous case where the same measure did not work.
items.WhyWrongE: No personal attack is made; the neighboring state is the evidence.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Editor: Some say we should stop printing a paper edition to cut costs. Yet our surveys show that half our subscribers would cancel entirely without print. Eliminating print would thus cost us more in lost subscriptions than it would save.
items.Stem: The editor responds to the cost-cutting proposal by
items.ChoiceA: attacking the competence of those who made the proposal.
items.ChoiceB: arguing that the proposal would produce a result that defeats its own purpose.
items.ChoiceC: proposing an alternative way to cut costs.
items.ChoiceD: conceding the proposal while rejecting the motives behind it.
items.ChoiceE: questioning the accuracy of the proposal's cost figures.
items.WhyWrongA: No attack on people is made.
items.WhyWrongB: CORRECT. The editor shows the cost-cutting move would raise costs, undermining its goal.
items.WhyWrongD: The editor rejects the proposal itself, not merely its motives.
items.WhyWrongE: The editor does not dispute the cost numbers.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Theorist A: Human decisions are entirely determined by prior causes. Theorist B: If that were true, no one could be held responsible for their actions. But we clearly do hold people responsible, and rightly so. Therefore, A's claim must be false.
items.Stem: Theorist B responds to Theorist A by
items.ChoiceA: appealing to the consensus of experts in the field.
items.ChoiceB: offering a single counterexample to A's general claim.
items.ChoiceC: presenting statistical evidence that contradicts A.
items.ChoiceD: showing that A's claim implies a consequence we reject, then rejecting the claim.
items.ChoiceE: redefining the notion of 'cause' to exclude decisions.
items.WhyWrongA: B appeals to a shared practice, not to experts.
items.WhyWrongB: B does not cite a specific counterexample; B derives an unacceptable implication.
items.WhyWrongD: CORRECT. This is a reductio: A implies no responsibility, which we deny, so A is false.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Researcher: Daily meditation reduces stress. In our study, participants who meditated every day for eight weeks reported significantly lower stress than a comparison group who did not meditate.
items.ChoiceA: defining stress in terms of measurable physical symptoms.
items.ChoiceB: generalizing from the experience of a single participant.
items.ChoiceC: drawing an analogy between meditation and other therapies.
items.ChoiceD: supporting a general claim by citing the results of a controlled comparison.
items.ChoiceE: appealing to the widespread popularity of meditation.
items.WhyWrongA: No definition of stress is offered.
items.WhyWrongB: The evidence is a group comparison, not one participant's experience.
items.WhyWrongC: No analogy to other therapies is drawn.
items.WhyWrongD: CORRECT. A general claim is backed by the outcome of a study comparing two groups.
items.WhyWrongE: Popularity plays no role in the reasoning.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Advocate: Running a city is like running a household. Just as a family must not spend more than it earns, a city must balance its budget every single year.
items.ChoiceA: citing statistics on the level of municipal debt.
items.ChoiceB: supporting a recommendation by drawing an analogy between two situations.
items.ChoiceC: defining what it means for a budget to be balanced.
items.ChoiceD: appealing to the testimony of an authority on economics.
items.ChoiceE: rejecting a proposal by pointing to its unintended effects.
items.WhyWrongB: CORRECT. A household analogy is used to support the prescription that a city balance its budget.
items.WhyWrongC: No definition of a balanced budget is provided.
items.WhyWrongE: The advocate advances a recommendation rather than rejecting a proposal.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Critic: The mayor claims that every neighborhood has benefited from the new transit plan. But the Riverside district has seen its bus service cut in half since the plan took effect.
items.Stem: The critic responds to the mayor's claim by
items.ChoiceA: showing that the mayor's premises contradict one another.
items.ChoiceB: questioning the mayor's motives for adopting the plan.
items.ChoiceC: challenging a general claim by offering a case that does not fit it.
items.ChoiceD: proposing a rival transit plan of the critic's own.
items.ChoiceE: arguing that the plan's costs exceed its benefits.
items.WhyWrongB: No motive is questioned; the focus is the factual claim.
items.WhyWrongC: CORRECT. The critic rebuts the universal claim by presenting Riverside as a counterexample.
items.WhyWrongE: The critic addresses coverage, not a cost-benefit balance.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Philosopher: Critics insist that art must be beautiful. But beauty and value are not the same thing. A work can be disturbing rather than beautiful and still be highly valuable as art. So the critics are wrong to require beauty.
items.ChoiceA: rebutting a position by distinguishing between two concepts it treats as equivalent.
items.ChoiceB: redefining 'art' so that it includes only valuable works.
items.ChoiceC: appealing to the authority of famous art critics.
items.ChoiceD: providing examples of art that is universally admired for its beauty.
items.ChoiceE: showing that the critics' view leads to a logical contradiction.
items.WhyWrongA: CORRECT. The philosopher pries apart beauty and value, which the critics conflate, to defeat their requirement.
items.WhyWrongB: 'Art' is not redefined; beauty and value are distinguished.
items.WhyWrongD: Such examples would support the critics, the opposite of the aim.
items.WhyWrongE: No contradiction is derived from the critics' view.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Coach: We will win the championship only if our defense improves. Our defense has not improved at all this season. So we will not win the championship.
items.ChoiceA: predicting a future outcome from a long-term statistical trend in the data.
items.ChoiceB: concluding that an outcome will not occur because a necessary condition for it has not been met.
items.ChoiceC: assuming that improved defense would by itself guarantee a win.
items.ChoiceD: attacking the abilities of the players on the opposing team.
items.ChoiceE: generalizing from the team's performance in past championship seasons.
items.WhyWrongB: CORRECT. The coach treats improved defense as necessary for winning and, finding it absent, denies the win.
items.WhyWrongC: The coach treats defense as necessary, not sufficient.
items.WhyWrongD: The opposing team is not discussed.
items.WhyWrongE: No appeal to past seasons is made.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Columnist: Admittedly, the new tax will raise prices slightly in the short term. But it will fund research that lowers production costs within five years, so consumers will ultimately pay less than they do now.
items.ChoiceA: claiming the tax is the only possible way to fund the research.
items.ChoiceB: denying that the tax will raise prices at all.
items.ChoiceC: granting a short-term drawback while arguing that a greater long-term benefit outweighs it.
items.ChoiceD: attacking the people who proposed the tax.
items.ChoiceE: citing an economist's published forecast of production costs.
items.WhyWrongA: No claim of uniqueness about funding is made.
items.WhyWrongB: The short-term price rise is conceded, not denied.
items.WhyWrongC: CORRECT. The columnist concedes a minor short-term cost, then argues the long-term savings outweigh it.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Ethicist: One should never make a promise one cannot keep. The senator promised to eliminate the entire deficit in a single year, which is plainly impossible. So the senator acted wrongly.
items.ChoiceA: questioning whether the size of the deficit can be measured.
items.ChoiceB: applying a general principle to a specific case in order to reach an evaluation.
items.ChoiceC: inferring a general rule from several specific cases.
items.ChoiceD: predicting that the senator will lose the next election.
items.ChoiceE: attacking the senator's character rather than the promise.
items.WhyWrongA: Measurement of the deficit is not at issue.
items.WhyWrongB: CORRECT. A stated principle about promises is applied to the senator's case to judge it wrong.
items.WhyWrongC: The reasoning runs from principle to case, not from cases to a rule.
items.WhyWrongD: No prediction about elections is made.
items.WhyWrongE: The promise, not the senator's character, is judged.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Ana: We should switch to the 'all-natural' skin-care line, because natural products are always safer. Ben: Many natural substances, such as certain plant toxins, are extremely dangerous. So being natural does not make a product safe.
items.ChoiceA: appealing to expert consensus about cosmetics.
items.ChoiceB: proposing a different standard for choosing skin-care products.
items.ChoiceC: showing that Ana's conclusion contradicts her own premise.
items.ChoiceD: undermining a premise by citing instances that violate the generalization it asserts.
items.ChoiceE: accusing Ana of deliberately misrepresenting the products.
items.WhyWrongC: Ben targets a premise, not an internal contradiction in Ana's reasoning.
items.WhyWrongD: CORRECT. Ben attacks the premise 'natural is always safer' with counterexamples of dangerous natural substances.
items.WhyWrongE: Ben disputes the claim, not Ana's honesty.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Philosopher: Some hold that because every event has a cause, no human choice is truly free. But this misses a distinction: an action caused by the agent's own reasoning can still be free. What removes freedom is not causation itself, but coercion by someone else. Universal causation therefore leaves room for free choice.
items.ChoiceA: presenting experimental evidence that people do in fact make genuinely free choices
items.ChoiceB: drawing a distinction between two kinds of cause to deny that causation as such supports the opposing conclusion
items.ChoiceC: attacking the motives of those people who deny that human choices are free
items.ChoiceD: granting that no human choice is really free while arguing that this hardly matters
items.ChoiceE: showing that the opponents' stated premises actually contradict one another
items.WhyWrongA: No empirical evidence is offered; the move is entirely conceptual.
items.WhyWrongB: CORRECT. The philosopher separates causation-by-one's-own-reasoning from coercion, denying that mere causation yields the no-freedom conclusion.
items.WhyWrongC: The opponents' motives are never discussed.
items.WhyWrongD: The philosopher rejects the conclusion rather than conceding it.
items.WhyWrongE: No internal contradiction in the opponents' premises is alleged.
